data.title: China’s official media calls for end to name-and-shame campaign against grass-roots cadres
data.description:
China’s official media has called on disciplinary watchdogs to stop giving low-level officials insulting nicknames that imply they are not doing their jobs properly.
The provincial publicity department of the eastern province of Zhejiang became the latest to weigh in on this issue. In a commentary posted on its official social media on Tuesday, it warned that this approach was likely to backfire.
It criticised the use of labels such as “refrigerator” officials to describe those who “don’t speak up, stay cold and don’t change”; “treadmill” types who “look busy and sweaty but who make no progress in their work”; or “lipstick” cadres who make things pretty on the surface without doing the in-depth work.
Others were accused of “lying flat” – a popular Chinese term to describe those who make minimal efforts – or “lying on their side”, referring to those who only act when given instructions.
The article said this “label-based evaluation … smacked of sensationalism” and warned that it was a barrier to properly understanding the dilemmas and problems grass-roots officials faced.
For example, it said those accused of lying flat might not necessarily be lazy but could be suffering from physical and mental exhaustion as a result of an unfair workload. Alternatively, it said, they might be worried that officials who took the initiative were at greater risk of making mistakes and being punished as a result.
It also expressed sympathy for those accused of lying on their side, saying they might face an overly strict authorisation process or have “nitpicking” superiors.
Beijing Daily, the capital’s official newspaper, published a similar commentary in early November, also calling for a halt to the use of such labels.
The current campaign began with another commentary published in August in China Organisation and Personnel Works News, a newspaper controlled by the Communist Party’s top personnel department.


The article warned against relying on name-and-shame tactics and said it was better to get to the bottom of cadres’ problems and offer them the help and support they needed.
Such tactics are in line with a drive from Beijing to tighten political control over the grass-roots, but the central authorities are trying to balance this with concerns that officials are struggling with poor morale and layers of bureaucracy.
Some local authorities have handed out “awards” to officials deemed to be performing poorly, sometimes publicly shaming them as well.
For example, a Sichuan television programme went viral at the end of last year when it showed several grass-roots officials from the city of Suining being forced to pose for photos with certificates branding them as “snails”, “hedgehogs” or “ostriches”.
Snails are seen as too slow to respond, hedgehogs as prickly and difficult to approach – in this case housing officials had been blamed for rejecting applications repeatedly – while the ostriches were accused of putting their heads in the sand and failing to show initiative.
Other cities, including Taizhou in eastern Jiangsu province and Zunyi in southwestern Guizhou, have handed out similar awards to officials deemed to have underperformed, ignored residents’ interests or failed to correct mistakes. Their annual performance bonuses were also cut.
But such campaigns have backfired by further harming morale, according to Alfred Wu, an associate professor at the National University of Singapore’s Lee Kuan Yew School of Public Policy.
“Many of these problems are caused by the further centralisation of decision-making power to the higher hierarchies, which leave the grass roots very limited room to make their own discretionary decisions,” Wu said.
Beijing has realised that there is a problem with morale and motivation and has taken a series of measures targeting the needless meetings low-level officials are forced to attend and the unnecessary reports they are expected to write.
In August, the party’s General Office and the State Council issued regulations to reduce red tape, streamline workflows and improve efficiency to ensure officials could devote more time to practical work.
The rules mandated strict control over the number of documents generated and meetings held, with content required to be concise and to the point. They also said virtual meetings should be favoured over in-person gatherings.

data.title: Updated Taiwan safety guide sparks fierce debate amid Beijing’s mounting military pressure
data.description:
Taiwan’s updated crisis and war survival guide has ignited fierce political debate, hailed by officials as a lifeline amid Beijing’s mounting military pressure but blasted by critics as costly propaganda prompting fear rather than readiness.
Mass distribution of In Case of Crisis: Taiwan’s National Public Safety Guide – quickly dubbed the “little orange book” – began late last month, targeting 9.83 million households.
The 29-page comic-style publication explains how to stockpile food and water, locate shelters and identify signs of escalating threats to the public, ranging from cyberattacks to a full-scale military assault.
It updates previous guidance by including information on landslide-dammed lakes following recent weather disasters, urging citizens to monitor warnings, heed evacuation orders and work with local authorities.
The foreword now carries Taiwanese leader William Lai Ching-te’s signature, a move the defence ministry said added “authority and credibility” and encouraged public trust.
Aside from offering guidance for natural disasters and extreme weather, the guide cites mainland China’s military threat as a direct challenge.

Beijing sees Taiwan as part of China to be reunited by force if necessary and has ramped up pressure on Taiwan since Lai took office last year, having labelled him a separatist.
Most countries, including the US, do not recognise Taiwan as an independent state, but Washington is opposed to any attempt to take the self-ruled island by force and is committed to supplying it with weapons.
The handbook details steps for finding air-raid shelters across Taiwan through mobile apps and preparing emergency kits, even for pets.
In addition, it highlights potential precursors to armed conflict such as sabotage of undersea cables, cyberwarfare and hostile forces patrolling around the island and demanding to board and inspect Taiwanese vessels.
It warns that “foreign hostile actors” could deploy deepfakes and disinformation to weaken public morale.
The handbook has added cybersecurity alerts on products made in mainland China, such as cameras and sensors. It singled out DeepSeek, WeChat, TikTok, Douyin and RedNote as potential risks for compromising personal data.
Perhaps most striking is one message printed in bold text: “If Taiwan comes under military invasion, any information about defeat or government surrender is fake news.”
Other new sections include advice for parents on discussing war with children and a chapter encouraging citizens to join civil-defence teams or even enlist as volunteers.

Officials said the update reflected a broader push for “whole-of-society resilience” as mainland Chinese military activity intensifies in the Taiwan Strait.
Lin Fei-fan, deputy secretary general of the island’s National Security Council, said “preparedness is the only way to deter aggression” and that “we are not provoking war” with the handbook.
Lin and other military officials have justified the large-scale distribution of copies by drawing comparisons with Nordic states such as Sweden and Finland and the Baltic country Estonia. All three have mass-mailed wartime survival manuals in recent years.
The officials said Tokyo regularly distributed disaster handbooks to millions of households and argued Taiwan was now acting “consistent with leading democratic nations”.
But one opinion poll suggested the roll-out had some way to go to win over the public.
A survey of more than 25,000 people, conducted on Wednesday by the app platform Line Today, revealed a significant split: 40 per cent of respondents felt the guide had “no practical effect”, compared with only 22 per cent who described it as “highly helpful”.
Smaller numbers of respondents called the handbook limited in usefulness or said they had yet to receive it.
Photos circulating online showed unopened hard copies tossed into recycling bins. One sarcastic comment read: “When missiles hit, scan the QR code, register your account – maybe win a lucky draw?”
Some in the online community voiced privacy concerns, citing past breaches in Taiwan’s government database.
A number of users said the booklet should have been digital rather than printed, though others countered that computer networks could be disabled in wartime.

At Neihu Technology Park in Taipei, workers told a local television channel they had never seen the booklet. After taking a brief look, one called it a “waste of taxpayer money”.
“Taiwan is tiny,” another said. “One artillery shell and we’re done. Where are we supposed to hide?”
Liu Shih-fang, Taiwan’s interior minister, slammed those who dismissed the handbook, saying such people must have “prince or princess syndrome”. Her comments sparked backlash.
Told of Liu’s rebuke, one person told a television reporter: “We’re overworked labourers, not pampered royalty. Stop spreading fear.”
Opposition lawmakers have seized on the guide’s mixed reception, accusing the ruling independence-leaning Democratic Progressive Party (DPP) of spending NT$65 million (US$2 million) on a vanity project.
The printing costs were covered by the cabinet’s emergency reserve fund – normally earmarked for natural disasters – instead of regular defence appropriations.
Wang Hung-wei, a legislator with the main opposition party Kuomintang (KMT), asked: “Is Taiwan preparing for war next year or simply burning cash before year-end?”
Fellow KMT lawmaker Hsu Chiao-hsin said it was “overly complicated to access shelter data”, for it required “scanning QR codes, downloading apps and registering accounts”.
Others complained that the handbook’s maps were unclear and that many actual shelters, including basements in government buildings, were not listed.

Lawmakers from the Taiwan People’s Party (TPP) likewise raised objections to the guide.
“Distributing it like an election flier creates waste and burdens local chiefs,” said TPP legislator Lin Kuo-cheng. “Why insist on paper when government pushes digitalisation?”
Fellow TPP legislator Mai Yu-chen echoed Lin’s criticism, saying “a survival manual must be usable, not a slogan book”.
But Lin of Taiwan’s National Security Council and military officials defended the decision, saying printed copies “ensure seniors and low-tech households are covered if power or networks fail”.
They insisted the handbook was just “one element of a wider resilience strategy” that included a multibillion-dollar budget to boost strategic stockpiles and strengthen infrastructure against blockade risks.
In the guide’s preface, Lai, Taiwan’s leader, wrote: “Our safety hinges on our will to defend ourselves and our readiness before crises occur.”
Meanwhile, the island’s defence ministry denied any intention to bypass legislative scrutiny or politicise the initiative.
Security analysts said the controversy reflected a broader societal divide over how to confront the possibility of armed conflict.
Former US Marine Michael Hunzeker, an associate professor at George Mason University in Virginia, praised the booklet’s clarity but argued its message was too soft.
“There’s almost no mention of war,” he told Taiwan’s Central News Agency.


“If I have one concrete recommendation, I do think the manual could be more forthright with the Taiwanese people, more transparent and honest with them about the nature of the military threat,” Hunzeker said.
Citizens needed psychological preparation for a worst-case scenario, he explained, warning that underestimating the risk could leave people paralysed in a crisis.
James Yi-fan Chen, a diplomacy professor at Tamkang University in New Taipei City, said the publication was in part “political propaganda at home and abroad”.
Chen believed the guide would be valuable in the event that digital access was lost but that it still lacked essential actionable information.
“Instead of simply distributing these booklets, Taiwanese citizens may need actual rehearsals to experience warlike scenarios,” he said.
“Otherwise, the guidebook may only heighten anxiety without giving people clear instructions on what to do.”
Lin of Taiwan’s National Security Council maintained that democracies had a responsibility to tell citizens how to survive disasters and conflict.
Amid increasingly sour cross-strait relations, local supporters of the “little orange book” said it was a necessary first step to prepare civilians for crises that now seemed less hypothetical.
For some observers and sceptics, however, it amounted to a hollow gesture serving political priorities more than practical defence and hinted at a DPP administration overstating the war threat to justify spending.

data.title: Politicisation of Hong Kong’s fire tragedy in Western media a new low
data.description:
As sure as night follows day, you can always count on the big Western media outlets to politicise anything bad that happens in Hong Kong or on the mainland. So with the horrific Tai Po fire, you can be sure those platforms will go to town with that approach.
The real cause of the fatal fire? Well, of course, it’s all about democracy and freedom, or rather the lack of their Western versions in China.
The same old “democracy” explains everything, which turns every ignorant foreign commentator into an expert. If you are disgusted by their sheer moral indecency, you are probably not alone.
Even as the bodies were still being recovered and identified, these Western “experts” already seemed to know what had happened. Are they so all-knowing or just reprehensibly presumptuous?
Here are some headlines: “Deadly Fire Highlights Hong Kong’s Predicament” (with the standfirst, “The tragedy confirmed to many locals that the city’s freedoms have vanished”), “The killer Hong Kong fire shows how freedom is an even greater loss than you’d think”, “Deadly Hong Kong fire is a test of Beijing’s rule in the city”.
In handling the aftermath of such a deadly fire, there is always room for improvement, and not every demand can be met immediately.
But the victims were immediately taken care of, including 294 of their pets rescued from the fire. The living allowance for every affected household has been doubled from HK$50,000 (US$6,410) to HK$100,000.
HK$2.8 billion in donations have already been raised from the government and private sources. Temporary housing is being provided. The needs of affected residents are being addressed in the short and medium term. The government has promised a long-term housing settlement, meaning each household is likely to be allocated a flat somewhere in the end.


Can democracy in Western countries offer comparably quick and comprehensive settlement and solutions? People should study how the US has handled its recurrent deadly hurricane crises or how the British government addressed the deadly Grenfell fire in London in 2017.
Some have compared the Wang Fuk Court fire with the Grenfell blaze, which claimed 72 lives, noting similarities such as both being subsidised housing, substandard material being used, and ignored complaints from residents. However, one American pundit has argued that the difference is that the British government launched a major public inquiry. “But unlike Grenfell,” he wrote, “where intense media scrutiny and a major public inquiry followed, such independent investigations are no longer possible in Hong Kong.”
Oh really? Britain’s then prime minister, Theresa May, announced an official inquiry on June 29 after the fire started on June 14, a time lag of more than two weeks. Hong Kong Chief Executive John Lee Ka-chiu ordered the establishment of an independent review committee, to be chaired by a judge, on Tuesday, within a week of the tragedy.
Yes, there have been crowd control measures, and the monitoring and arrests of people suspected of committing fraud or of hate-mongering by spreading malicious rumours and conspiracy theories.
Don’t they do that in Western democracies? People have been arrested and jailed in Britain and the United States for much less.

While covering the aftermath in Tai Po, a colleague witnessed some so-called volunteers handing out masks to mourners while falsely claiming that cyanide and other poisonous substances were being emitted from the charred buildings. Many people went on to put on masks.
Rumours and scams are on the rise. That’s to be expected when there is so much money involved. Malicious individuals have been seen carrying cards warning people from the mainland to stay away and not to try to steal donations for the victims.
That the police have made arrests or detained some for questioning has been immediately seized on by some foreign media outlets as evidence of a government cover-up.
The local press has been much more responsible and circumspect. This again has been interpreted in some quarters as a sign of political censorship.
After the Grenfell tragedy, rumours swirled. The BBC and other responsible news outlets took pains to verify their reports, checking with the government and other official sources. That’s just journalistic professionalism, which aims to report the facts. Sadly, many Western news media platforms seem incapable of not politicising any bad news coming out of China.
Hong Kong is far from perfect. As the city responds to this terrible tragedy, more mistakes may be made along the way. But we are more than capable of taking care of our own without being badmouthed by ill-wishers.

data.title: Thai woman stays on rooftop with pets for days during floods, refusing rescue without them
data.description:
A Thai woman declined rescue and chose to remain stranded on a rooftop amid severe floods to stay with her pet dog and cat, garnering online praise.
The woman, known as Jazz, appealed for help on social media on November 22.
She reported being trapped on the roof with the water level rising dangerously close to her.
In her post, she also provided her location and requested authorities to cut power in her neighbourhood to prevent electrocution.
In a video included with her post, she was seen at a makeshift tent on the rooftop, accompanied by her cat and dog.

Torrential rains caused catastrophic floods, the worst in years, across Southeast Asian countries. Over 1.4 million households and 3.8 million people have been affected by flooding in 12 provinces of southern Thailand.
Throughout the 30 hours she was stranded, Jazz continued to post pleas for rescue.
A reporter who shared her situation, which received 58,000 likes, noted that rescuers arrived earlier by boat during the heavy rain but could not take her dog with them.
Jazz refused to leave without her dog, while expressing understanding of the rescue teams’ safety protocols. She later fitted a muzzle on the dog, but the rescuers did not return.
She mentioned that her cat was too frightened to leave the pet carrier.
Fortunately, Jazz and her two pets managed to survive the ordeal on their own.

As the water level began to recede, she resolved to make her way out by herself.
At 10am the following day, she posted again, sharing a photo of herself wading through knee-high water, her dog secured in a plastic bucket.
She confirmed that both her pets and she were safe and expressed gratitude to online supporters for their concern. She added that she had been starving after not eating anything for three days.
Reflecting on her choice to stay with her pets, she stated: “People who do not like animals will never understand. Dogs and cats have lives too. We are their entire world.”

One commenter said: “I am so happy to know you are safe now. I respect you for not abandoning your pets.”
Another praised Jazz’s decision, saying: “Animals are of equal value to humans because they breathe the same air as us.”
Some commenters even offered to donate food for her pets.
One individual showed understanding towards the rescue workers’ decision not to return, stating: “I understand the rescue workers from my experience working with them. They had to avoid dangerous areas even though they wanted to help. They are outnumbered by the victims, and they also have their own families to care for,” the person said.

data.title: Hong Kong’s young pickleball scene showcased as city hosts leg of global tour
data.description:
Organisers of the World Pickleball Championships in Hong Kong said they aimed to use their event as a platform to bigger and better things as the action got under way on Friday.
The city’s leg of the annual touring series, which will run until Sunday, boasts only about 25 professionals, mostly locals, among a total field of nearly 900 players.
But it represents an important step in capitalising on growing interest in the shorter and more accessible version of tennis, according to Barry Lau, co-founder and chairman of event organiser LIT TLP Pickleball.
While players from at least 16 countries and regions are taking part at Discovery Bay Recreation Club, for Lau and his colleagues, it is just as important to push local participation.

“Pickleball is still very young in Hong Kong,” Lau said. “There are still too few professional pickleball players in Hong Kong. Only by developing more professionals and bringing them to world-class tournaments can we promote Hong Kong in the sport.”
Lau’s company has so far signed three full-time players – paying salaries and all expenses – and intends to unearth more.
“We definitely need new athletes,” LIT TLP’s president Rocky Chan Man-chun said. “There are not many now, but there will definitely be more in the future.”
The city’s improving facilities would help to drive that, he said.
“Of course, if there are better venues, we can host higher-level tournaments and attract more international players,” Chan said. “There were big challenges for this event’s courts at the start, but with the help of Discovery Bay Recreation Club, we now have 25 courts.”
There are four brand new professional pickleball courts in use at this weekend’s event, plus two tennis courts modified for both tennis and pickleball, and 19 temporary courts created by marking lines on tennis courts. All meet tournament standards.
“Of course, having dedicated pickleball courts is best,” said Ryan Lam Chun-hei, a full-time pickleball player from Hong Kong who has often seen international tournaments use adapted tennis courts.
“Actually, being able to play in Discovery Bay with such good weather and environment is already very good in Hong Kong,” he said.
“In other places like Malaysia and Vietnam, there are already many dedicated courts, but Hong Kong has less space and it is more difficult to build such venues. But now we have them, and we will gradually see more and more.”

This is Lam’s third year playing pickleball. He recalls initially having to play on football pitches with lines taped down, but that is changing.
“This year, there are really more professional courts, especially in the past six months,” he said.
“We need more people to participate in matches and in the sport, so that pickleball can be better promoted.”

data.title: ‘Like a ghost town’: Hong Kong neighbourhood struggles to adjust after fatal fire
data.description:
Hong Kong’s deadliest fire in decades has been put out, but the sight of the charred remains of Wang Fuk Court’s housing blocks still conjures up painful emotions and all too vivid memories for many people in the surrounding neighbourhood.
The fire started on Wednesday last week, raging for 43 hours and engulfing seven of the estate’s eight towers before it was extinguished. At least 159 people died in the inferno.
On Friday, residents from the nearby Kwong Fuk Estate public housing complex were seen gathering along a platform at the housing complex.
Some were occasionally spotted standing at the far end, between Kwong Yan House and Kwong Yau House, looking at what was left of Wang Fuk Court.
The number of police vehicles parked outside Kwong Fuk Market halved from the day before, while a section of Plover Cove Road that led into the estate was unblocked in the afternoon.
For many in the area surrounding Wang Fuk Court, life began returning to something like its usual pace. But some could not help but feel that nothing would ever be the same again.
Tsang, a homemaker living in Kwong Fuk Estate’s Kwong Lai House, said the atmosphere was no longer the same. The resident, who is in her forties, moved to the 42-year-old estate seven years ago.
“I didn’t feel the resentment in the air as social media users have suggested, but it surely felt gloomy, quiet and desolate when I was walking around at night. It’s just completely different,” she said.
“With no one living there [at Wang Fuk Court] now, it’s a lot darker too. It feels like a ghost town. There are some temporary lights tied everywhere, but I assume they will be removed eventually.”
She asked a Post reporter if he could appeal to the government to “put up more permanent lamp posts around the area”.

A 70-year-old man, who only gave his name as Shum, said he had lived at Kwong Fuk Estate since its completion in 1983 and described the tragedy as having had a psychological effect on him.
“There’s no way to calm down, really, because the whole thing emerged right in front of me,” he said.
“Every morning when I wake up, I am looking at those heavily burnt buildings [in the direction of Tai Po Baptist Public School].
“I see those charred buildings at home. I still see them while going downstairs to eat or walk around. There’s no forgetting this, to be honest. I have a relative who lives at Wang Chi House and is safe, but it’s not about people you know or not, it’s about everyone.”
Since the fire, residents have also gathered at the Kwong Fuk Estate’s outdoor sitting area to mourn the victims.
On two occasions, the Post reporter was approached by people asking where they could leave flowers for the dead.


Construction worker Chan Wai-shing spent his lunch break sitting along the Kwong Fuk platform and staring at the adjacent charred housing blocks. He lamented that the fire had taken “many innocent lives”.
“I was working at a Civil Engineering and Development Department [CEDD] site on Wong Yi Au Road last week; it was about an eight to 10-minute walk from here,” he said.
“At about 4pm Wednesday [last week], from where I was, we could still hear people crying and yelling ‘Help!’ from those buildings. I knew it was very serious. How desperate they must have been to scream like that and for us to hear from afar?
“No sadness is evoked by looking at the scene personally; it’s already been the seventh day [since the deaths]. I hope we can all move on.”
Chan was referring to Taoist and Buddhist beliefs that on the seventh day, the souls of the deceased revisit their loved ones in the mortal world to say a final goodbye.
He said that, having worked for half a decade in construction, residential buildings undergoing massive renovation works might not always have supervisors on-site.
“Unlike the government sites under CEDD, we have a more casual weekly check, and a more complete, formal check every month, where the owner, engineers, architects and government officials would carry out spot checks,” the 70-year-old said.
“You don’t know which part of the site they are going to check; it could be anywhere, so you cannot change anything at all in an instant; it feels safer.”

Chan is also a “Technically Competent Person”, a status granted under a Construction Industry Council registration scheme for qualified professionals overseeing construction works. He renewed his qualification in October, with the status valid until March 2031.
He said then Urban Renewal Authority chief executive Wai Chi-sing, back in 2021, had wanted to ensure there were enough registered personnel stationed on construction sites, but Chan noted there were just “not enough” qualified persons available.
“Some sites had offered me to station at their sites for three days a week, but they only required me to arrive with 30 minutes’ notice and didn’t allow me to enter the construction site,” he said.
“That’s why I never work for them. I need to have full access to the site if I am to be a supervisor on-site for them. I need to know all those safety measures are up to standard.”
Kwong Fuk Estate also attracts those left bereaved by the fire, such as Frances Ng.
She said an elderly relative had lived on one of the lower floors of Wang Fuk Court’s Wang Cheong House with a domestic helper.
Ng said she was convinced a charred body found in the flat was that of her relative, but the helper was still missing.
“We still haven’t found her, and haven’t gone [to the community centre] to further check those photo albums [for the deceased]. There were no updates for us from the police,” the 35-year-old said.
“We are in contact with our missing helper’s sister, who also works in Hong Kong. We will see what we can do for her, but perhaps it’s time to deal with the other matters first, for now.”

data.title: How China’s history of fighting fire reflects a long and difficult struggle
data.description:
It will be a sombre December for Hong Kong as the city recovers from the shock of the massive fire that engulfed seven high-rise residential blocks in Tai Po last week, the deadliest fire in Hong Kong in decades.
At the time of writing, 159 people are confirmed dead and dozens more are injured. Few would remain unmoved by the distraught tears of victims who lost family members and their homes to the blaze.
Fire was a constant threat in premodern Chinese cities, where most buildings were made of wood and built with tightly joined timber frames.
The very qualities that made these structures beautiful and utilitarian also made them dangerously flammable. Dense roof structures trapped heat and smoke, and good ventilation – often necessary for comfort – fed flames instead of stopping them.
Unsurprisingly, successive dynasties invested enormous effort in preventing fires.
Some measures were purely symbolic. Perched on the ends of roof ridges, for example, could be found decorative wenshou: mythical water creatures believed to ward off evil and, in keeping with their aquatic nature, fire.

Similarly, the names of buildings were considered important, especially those housing precious, highly flammable items like books. When the Complete Library of the Four Treasuries, a massive encyclopaedia commissioned by the Qing dynasty’s Qianlong Emperor, was completed in 1782, copies were housed in seven imperial libraries, six of which featured the radical for “water” in their names. The seventh was excluded because it stood on the banks of the Yangtze River.
Alongside symbolic precautions came more concrete methods. As early as the Tang dynasty (618-907), records describe water-filled animal skin bags that were squeezed by several strong men to create rudimentary high-pressure jets to put out fires.
By the Northern Song (960-1127), firefighting technology had become slightly more hi-tech. Sources from the period list water bladders made from animal hides or the stomachs of pigs or cattle, manually operated pumps using perforated bamboo tubes, and “hemp mops” – long poles weighted with water-soaked hemp and mud – that were used to beat back flames.
During his time governing the city of Hangzhou, Su Shi (1037-1101) even established an official fire brigade equipped with ropes, axes, saws, signal flags and protective clothing.
During the Ming dynasty (1368-1644), firefighting became more institutionalised. Major urban centres maintained proper fire stations stocked with buckets, ladders and rudimentary hoses.
The imperial palaces of the Ming and Qing (1644-1912) dynasties were guarded by elite troops who doubled as fire wardens; by the time of the early Qing, Beijing’s Forbidden City had a specialised fire brigade made up of several hundred men. Their main tool was the “water dragon” (shuilong), a hose connected to a sophisticated hand-operated pump.

The most visually striking fire-prevention measure of all was static, as seen in the hundreds of massive water vats positioned throughout the Forbidden City. Each held over 3,000 litres (800 gallons) of water and required daily upkeep. In summer, palace eunuchs ensured the water stayed clean, and in winter, they wrapped the vats in quilted covers and kept charcoal fires glowing beneath to prevent freezing.
In the late Qing, foreign technology began to supplement traditional equipment. In 1887, Hangzhou bought a Western-style wheeled pump, nicknamed the “foreign dragon” (yanglong), and engaged a dedicated crew to operate it. By the 1890s, local authorities owned several such machines, alongside ladders, ropes and axes.
Most of these past measures were, however, largely ineffective, especially in large, heavily built-up cities.
Hong Kong’s recent tragedy is a grim reminder that fires are an enduring menace. From symbolic safeguards to modern fire safety regulations, China’s long history of fire prevention and firefighting reflects a constant struggle to confront the destructive force that still haunts cities today.

data.title: The enduring legacy of bamboo, a symbol of resilience, strength and adaptability in Asia
data.description:
Bamboo, a subfamily of typically fast-growing, tall, tree-like grasses, is found in tropical, subtropical and mild temperate regions across almost all continents, except Antarctica and Europe. In prehistoric South, East and Southeast Asia, it is well known for its use in tools, weapons, means of construction, transport and food.
Due to its characteristics of bending without breaking and remaining evergreen, bamboo symbolises resilience, strength and adaptability, as well as prosperity and fertility. The ancient Chinese poetry collection Book of Songs contains numerous references to bamboo symbolising family prosperity.
Europeans’ encounters with the plant and its name during their age of exploration are first attested in the writings of Portuguese doctor, herbalist and naturalist Garcia de Orta, who worked primarily in Goa and Bombay in Portuguese India.
In his 1563 Coloquios dos Simples, the earliest European treatise on the medicinal and economic plants of India, he writes of “aquellas canas daquella arvore chamam os Indios, onde nasce, mambu”, describing “the canes of that tree the Indians, where it grows, call mambu”.
Similarly, bamboe is documented in the 1598 writings of Dutch merchant traveller J.H. van Linschoten, as (in the English translation) “a thicke reede, as big as a mans legge, which is called Bambus”.

While some point to Sumatran Malay, Sundanese and Javanese bambu, or an old Malay word samambu, as the source of this word in European languages, many accounts concur that the ultimate origin lies in the Dravidian language Kannada bănbŭ or banwu.
“Bamboo” lends itself to several concepts, given its strong associations with East Asia.
The “bamboo curtain” – coined by analogy to Europe’s Iron Curtain – refers to the political and economic barrier, from 1949 to 1972, between communist territories of East Asia, particularly the People’s Republic of China, and non-Communist countries.
The “bamboo network” conceptualises the vast constellation of businesses owned and run by overseas Chinese in Southeast Asia – notably the Min/Hokkien communities – which has flourished for more than a century, tracing back to the mass migrations of Chinese labourers and traders during the Qing dynasty (1644-1912) and the colonial period. Built on familial ties, trust and values, the network links the region to the economies of Greater China.
The “bamboo ceiling” is an offshoot of the “glass ceiling”, both metaphors for systemic barriers in the workplace.
The “glass ceiling”, coined in the 1970s, refers to the invisible barrier that prevents women from advancing to higher positions because of their gender. Extending this concept, the “bamboo ceiling”, coined in 2005, references the combination of individual, cultural and organisational factors that impede the career progress of Asian-Americans, particularly in reaching senior leadership positions.

In Chinese languages, the concept of bamboo – zūk (竹) in Cantonese, or zhū in Mandarin – generally evokes positive traits such as steadiness and resilience.
An interesting exception is found in Cantonese, where a term with more negative connotations exists: zūk sīng (竹升). The term refers to a bamboo pole, describing someone who is more Western than Chinese, due to having been born or raised or having spent a significant amount of time overseas and being devoid of Chinese culture and values, much like a hollow bamboo pole.
The original term for a bamboo pole was actually zūk gong (竹杠). However, the second character is homophonous with the character gong (降), which has the potentially inauspicious meaning of “fall” or “decrease”. Thus, this character was replaced with its opposite: sīng (升), meaning “rise”.
Bamboo scaffolding – zūk pàahng (竹棚) in Cantonese – has been made a scapegoat by some commentators for the devastating fire that razed Wang Fuk Court in Hong Kong’s Tai Po district last week.
The practice of bamboo scaffolding, which dates back millennia in China, has long been the most iconic of construction materials and practices in Hong Kong, characterising the city’s urban landscape.
Its particular use for constructing temporary Cantonese opera stages – Bamboo Theatre Building Technique – was inscribed on the Hong Kong Representative List of Intangible Cultural Heritage in 2017.

data.title: Why the Philippines can’t escape its endless cycle of disaster
data.description:
The tarpaulin barely held against the wind, sagging under days of ceaseless rain.
For the earthquake survivors of San Remigio, in the central Philippines, it was all that remained – a thin sheet of plastic above a patch of mud, standing in for the homes a 6.9-magnitude quake had reduced to rubble weeks earlier.
Then, in early November, Typhoon Kalmaegi barrelled through.
The storm, known locally as Tino, drenched thousands of families still huddled in their makeshift shelters. Roads vanished beneath floodwater. Power lines snapped and stayed down. Aid was stranded on the wrong side of washed-out highways. For many, hope felt as precarious as the tarpaulin pinned above their heads.

“One piece of tarpaulin the size of a plywood sheet for a whole family, that was all they had,” said Mayfourth Luneta, deputy executive director of the Center for Disaster Preparedness. “If you have five family members, you’re basically just standing under it.”
Luneta had flown from Manila to central Cebu province after the quake, only to find herself trapped in the chaos of a second disaster. Kalmaegi struck just as her team was due to deliver relief. The main highway out of Mandaue City turned into a river.
“People were shocked because that area doesn’t usually flood like that, not to the extent we were hearing about,” she said.
When her team finally found a way through, they discovered communities pushed past the point of desperation. Some had been without electricity for weeks. Others lined the sides of roads, begging for help. “Everywhere we went, they kept saying, ‘Thank you so much! We’ve been without electricity for so long!’”
We’re seeing three typhoons ravage the country in a week
Mayfourth Luneta, Filipino aid worker
For aid workers like Luneta, what is unfolding in the Philippines no longer feels like a series of isolated emergencies. It feels like a punishing new normal.
“We used to see one or two strong typhoons a year. Now it feels like three in a week,” she said.
This convergence of back-to-back storms, worsening floods and overlapping disasters offers a stark preview of life on the front lines of global warming, scientists say. Disasters that once seemed rare and extraordinary now crash into one another, erasing the space between recovery and the next emergency.
For communities already struggling with poverty, weak infrastructure and systemic corruption, each shock piles onto the last, creating the sense of a seemingly endless cycle of emergencies.

In Marikina City, which endures serious flooding almost every year, Philippine Red Cross administrator Jennifer de Guzman is accustomed to the grim routine of disaster response.
“But the situation in Cebu during Typhoon Tino felt different,” said the aid worker who joined a 10-day relief operation after the storm had hit.
“Even though we were familiar with the rhythm of emergency operations, the sheer scale of the destruction in Cebu demanded more from us: more coordination, more manpower, more time.”
The damage spanned a wide area, forcing a bigger, more agile response. In Marikina, years of repeated floods have bred local know-how and hard-won resilience, but Cebu showed how even seasoned responders can become overwhelmed when multiple overlapping disasters arrive all at once.


Super typhoons such as Haiyan in 2013, one of the most destructive tropical cyclones ever recorded, were once treated as once-in-a-generation calamities.
“But now, we’re seeing three typhoons ravage the country in a week,” Luneta said. “We have been feeling the worsening effects of climate change on disasters in the Philippines first hand.”
“It wasn’t this severe before … From the neglect we’ve shown in the destruction of the environment, we are now harvesting this in heaps of disasters,” she told This Week in Asia.
Clusters of cyclones
Scientists and front‑line responders warn that climate change is dramatically altering the types, frequency and severity of disasters hitting the Philippines.
Gwendolyn Pang, secretary general of the Philippine Red Cross, said the changes were now unmistakable. “There has been a notable increase in both the frequency and intensity of these tropical cyclones,” she told This Week in Asia.
Last year, the Southeast Asian nation saw a rare cluster of six cyclones hit in less than five weeks. This year, two super typhoons struck within days of each other.
And typhoons are only part of the story. Regions that were once protected from major flooding are now being hit by heavy monsoon rains and unexpected storms, putting extra pressure on relief teams.

Pang said the country’s geography of scattered islands made relief work all the more difficult, so agencies were now focusing on preparing for disasters before they happened.
The Philippines has weathered a succession of powerful typhoons in recent years, including Bopha in 2012, Haiyan in 2013 and Rai in 2021.
New climate studies are beginning to quantify what communities have long sensed. Researchers at Imperial College London’s Grantham Institute have found that climate change intensified the destructive power of Kalmaegi and Fung‑wong – typhoons known locally as Tino and Uwan that struck within days of each other last month, killing more than 250 people.
Human-induced climate change had increased both wind speeds and rainfall, making storms of this magnitude “more frequent, dangerous and costly because of the continued use of fossil fuels”, said institute director Ralf Toumi.
Lessons from Japan
The Philippines sits squarely in the Pacific Typhoon Belt and the West Pacific Cyclone Basin, two of the world’s most disaster-prone zones where extreme weather is common.
Japan faces similar dangers from storms and earthquakes, but has been able to lower its risk through better planning and preparedness. The Philippines ranks as the country with the highest disaster risk globally, while Japan is ranked 17th, according to the 2025 World Risk Index published by Germany’s Development Aid Alliance and the Institute for International Law of Peace and Armed Conflict.
“This shows that risk is not the same as hazard. Risk must be managed,” said geologist Mahar Lagmay, a professor at the University of the Philippines.

“If you can manage the exposure of people and communities, control development and strengthen the capacities of the population, you can lower the risk.”
“Japan is far more effective at this,” he added, citing the East Asian nation’s advanced planning, ample resources and proven ability to minimise risks despite also facing severe hazards, including snow-related dangers.
“Japan’s greater resources allow it to reduce vulnerability,” Lagmay said. “The Philippines, by contrast, has far less capacity.”
Despite living with typhoons, floods and earthquakes as a matter of course, the Philippines is still ill prepared for the scale of devastation now routinely unfolding, experts say.
We are trapped in a cycle of merely preparing, but we can no longer truly defend ourselves
Ven Paolo Valenzuela, Filipino researcher
“We are trapped in a cycle of merely preparing, but we can no longer truly defend ourselves against disasters,” said Ven Paolo Valenzuela, a Filipino research fellow at Singapore Management University who specialises in disaster risk reduction and climate change adaptation.
“It’s as if we’re just waiting to get punched and we were never taught how to dodge.”
For Valenzuela, the clearest example of this gap is the country’s inability to pass a national land-use law – a basic framework for deciding where people should live, where businesses and infrastructure can safely be built, and which areas are simply too dangerous for permanent settlement.
“Land-use classification is absolutely critical for a risk-informed development pathway,” he said. “Which areas are exposed to natural hazards? Which are safe? Which areas should have residents because they are safe, and which can we afford to develop with some calculated risk?”

Without such a framework, even the best disaster-risk data could not meaningfully shape how cities expanded or where roads, bridges and industrial zones were sited, Valenzuela added.
Since Haiyan struck in 2013, the Philippines has made progress in sharing hazard information and raising public awareness. But much of the scientific knowledge is still seen as academic research rather than being used in everyday planning and decision-making.
One of the most ambitious efforts was Project Noah, short for Nationwide Operational Assessment of Hazard, which was launched in 2012 to provide detailed, real-time hazard maps and warnings for evacuations and rescue efforts.
Noah combined global science and advanced technology such as Lidar 3D scanning to map risks, model floods and identify danger zones. Scientists credit it with helping to cut the Philippines’ weather-related deaths by almost two-thirds between 2014 and 2024.

But Noah was shut down in 2017 due to funding cuts, moving to the University of the Philippines in a much reduced role, focusing on helping local governments make science-based plans.
The loss of national-scale innovation, especially the Lidar programme, was deeply felt – had it continued, the country could have mapped far more, built stronger disaster resilience and potentially saved more lives, said Lagmay, who was the project director.
“We could have done so much more … we could have probably saved more lives,” he said. “We could have built an army of disaster researchers. We could have inspired more. We could have used more technologies.”
Billions washed away
Compounding the Philippines’ vulnerabilities is a problem that predates climate change but now magnifies its toll: corruption.
A widening flood-control corruption scandal has seen numerous lawmakers and local officials accused of colluding with contractors to build substandard, overpriced or outright non‑existent “ghost” projects, including infrastructure meant to protect communities from the very storms that are now arriving with growing ferocity.
Since 2022, the administration of President Ferdinand Marcos Jnr has disbursed a total of 545 billion pesos (US$9.2 billion) for flood-control projects. Yet Metro Manila and nearby areas still spent days submerged under deep, foul-smelling floodwaters following torrential rains this year.
Valenzuela traced the rot back decades, pointing to corruption in flood-control and related infrastructure in the 1990s and 2000s, when weak dykes were built after the deadly eruption of Mount Pinatubo in 1991.

“What makes it worse now is that climate change is delivering more intense hazards every year,” he said. “For every peso stolen, the equivalent cost is hundreds or thousands more in disaster damage, lost lives and lost futures.”
For Luneta, the effect is painfully visible on the ground, where responders are forced to stretch limited relief across an ever denser calendar of emergencies.
“We’re all fighting for meagre resources,” she said, adding that people had started to connect “the corruption issues … to the devastation they experience each year.”
Valenzuela drew a stark contrast between the Philippines and its regional peers. He slammed the country’s flood control projects as patchwork efforts that failed to match the level of risk they were supposed to manage.
He urged Philippine officials to look to neighbours such as Taiwan, Japan and Singapore, which treat disaster‑prevention works as core development infrastructure, not optional add‑ons.
“When they invest properly in coastal defences and flood control, even a strong typhoon becomes just another rainy day,” he said.
“Because our infrastructure keeps failing us … We are left to fend for ourselves and prepare on our own every single time.”

data.title: Trump talks trade with Canada, Mexico leaders at World Cup draw
data.description:
US President Donald Trump met with the leaders of Mexico and Canada to discuss trade issues on Friday after the leaders hosted the 2026 World Cup draw at Washington’s storied Kennedy Centre.
The White House said Trump held talks with Canadian Prime Minister Mark Carney and Mexican President Claudia Sheinbaum after the star-studded event, but gave no details on their discussions.
Canadian media said the meeting lasted for 45 minutes and the leaders spoke on their own, with no staff present.
Trump was upbeat about the talks before the meetings, saying trade and immigration would be on the agenda.
“We’re going to meet with both, and we’re getting along very well,” Trump told reporters as he arrived for the event.

Trump chatted with Carney and Sheinbaum as they watched one of the biggest events in the sporting world from a box at the Washington arts venue, after the trio appeared on a game show-like stage together to “draw” their individual countries’ names.
Trump, who has campaigned aggressively this year for a Nobel Peace Prize, was given an inaugural peace prize by Fifa for his efforts to promote dialogue and de-escalation in some of the world’s biggest hotspots.
The meeting came as the United States, Mexico and Canada prepare for a mandatory 2026 formal review of the US-Mexico-Canada trade agreement amid rising US tariffs and calls by Washington to renegotiate or possibly withdraw from the pact.
It was Sheinbaum’s first in-person meeting with Trump since she took office in October last year.
Trump has been broadly complimentary of the Mexican leader while pushing the country to do more to combat drug cartels and the flow of narcotics such as fentanyl to the United States.
He has vowed to maintain a 25 per cent US tariff on Mexican goods that are not compliant with the USMCA, pending more progress in the drug fight.

Relations have been more tense with Canada.
Trump had an amicable meeting with Carney in October, but then suspended all trade talks with Canada after the province of Ontario aired an advertisement using video of former US president Ronald Reagan warning that tariffs lead to trade wars and economic disaster.
Trump also threatened to increase duties on Canadian imports, though he has yet to follow through.
USMCA, which replaced the North American Free Trade Agreement in 2020 and was negotiated during Trump’s first term as president, requires the three countries to hold a joint review after six years.
Trump could decide next year to withdraw from the trade agreement, Politico reported on Thursday, citing US Trade Representative Jamieson Greer.
Greer did not repeat the comment at an event later on Thursday, telling an interviewer that USMCA was the law of the land, a law passed by Congress, and that Canada and Mexico were the United States’ largest export destinations.
